specification.topic-statement: The SSE parser processes a stream of raw byte chunks into discrete, parsed Server-Sent Events.

scope.in-scope: Parser creation, data accumulation, event extraction, and done-marker detection.
scope.boundary: The caller delivers raw byte chunks of any size, in any order, at any time. The parser makes no assumptions about chunk boundaries. The source of those bytes — HTTP response body, file, pipe — is outside scope.
The caller receives extracted events and decides what to do with them. How events are interpreted, routed, or acted upon is outside scope.

field.name: buffer
field.note: accumulates all fed data; null-terminated; grows but never shrinks

field.name: current length
field.note: number of valid bytes in the buffer

field.name: capacity
field.note: total allocated size of the buffer

field.name: event type
field.note: null when no event: field appeared in the event block

field.name: data
field.note: null when no data: field appeared in the event block

behavior.parser-creation:
A new parser is created with an internal buffer pre-allocated at 4096 bytes. The buffer is zeroed, the current length is set to zero, and the buffer is null-terminated. The capacity is recorded as 4096.
If memory allocation fails at any point during creation, the process aborts immediately. No error is returned.
behavior.data-accumulation:
Raw byte data of a specified length is appended to the parser's internal buffer.
If the specified length is zero, the operation completes immediately with no change to the buffer, regardless of what the data pointer contains.
Before appending, the parser checks whether the current buffer can hold the incoming data plus a null terminator. If not, the capacity is doubled repeatedly until the buffer is large enough. The buffer is then reallocated at the new capacity. If reallocation fails, the process aborts immediately. The buffer never shrinks — capacity only increases.
The incoming bytes are copied verbatim into the buffer immediately after any existing data. The current length is increased by the number of bytes fed. A null terminator is written at the new end position, maintaining the buffer as a valid C string even though the content may contain arbitrary bytes.
behavior.event-extraction:
The parser scans its buffer for the earliest event-terminating delimiter and, when one is found, extracts and returns a parsed event. If no complete event is available, nothing is returned.
Two delimiter forms are recognized: a pair of line feeds (`

`, consuming 2 bytes) and a pair of CRLF sequences (`

`, consuming 4 bytes). The parser searches for both simultaneously.
The delimiter that appears earliest in the buffer is used. When only one form is present, that form is used. When both are present, the one at a lower byte offset wins. If neither delimiter is present, nothing is returned and the buffer is unchanged.
The two-byte sequence `

` does not contain `

` as a substring, because the character at offset 2 within `

` is ``, not `
`. The two searches are therefore always independent and never refer to overlapping positions.
Once a delimiter is chosen, the event text is defined as all bytes before the delimiter's starting position. The consumed region — event text plus delimiter — is removed from the buffer. Any bytes that follow the delimiter are shifted to the beginning of the buffer, and the current length is updated accordingly. Capacity is not changed.
The event text is split into lines by scanning for `
` characters only. Each line is classified by its prefix:
A line at least 6 characters long that begins with the literal text `event:` causes the event type field to be set. The characters immediately after `event:` form the value. If the first of those characters is a space, exactly one space is stripped. All remaining characters up to the end of the line become the event type value. If the event block contains multiple `event:` lines, each one replaces the previous value; the last `event:` line in the block determines the final event type.
Line splitting uses only `
` as a separator. If a line ends with `
` (CRLF), the `` is included in the computed line length and therefore becomes part of the extracted event type value. For example, an event block terminated by `

` whose internal lines are also CRLF-terminated will produce an event type that ends with a carriage return character.
A line at least 5 characters long that begins with the literal text `data:` contributes to the data payload. The characters immediately after `data:` form the content. If the first of those characters is a space, exactly one space is stripped. All remaining characters up to the end of the line are the content for this line.
The first `data:` line in a block initializes the data payload to that content. Each subsequent `data:` line appends a single `
` character followed by the new content to the payload. The final data value is the concatenation of all data lines, joined by `
`.
A `data:` line with no characters after the colon (or only a single space that is stripped) contributes an empty string to the payload. If it is the first data line, the payload is initialized to an empty string. If it is a subsequent line, a `
` followed by an empty string is appended (adding a trailing newline to the existing payload).
The same carriage return inclusion described for event-field lines applies to data lines when internal lines use CRLF endings.
Any line that does not meet the `event:` prefix condition and does not meet the `data:` prefix condition is silently ignored. This includes lines shorter than 5 characters, comment lines beginning with `:`, and lines beginning with any other field name such as `id:` or `retry:`.
When the delimiter appears at the very start of the buffer (event text length is zero), the extracted event has a null event type and null data. No lines are processed.
The returned event is allocated on the caller-provided memory context. Its event type field and data field, when present, are also owned by that same event object and are released when the event is released. The event type is null when no `event:` line appeared in the block. The data is null when no `data:` line appeared in the block.
behavior.done-marker-detection:
An event is tested to determine whether it signals end-of-stream.
If the event's data field is null, the result is false.
If the event's data field is exactly the 6-character string `[DONE]` (case-sensitive, with no leading or trailing whitespace), the result is true.
For any other data value, the result is false.
